Fully Homomorphic Encryption: Computing on Secrets

2 min read · 477 words · Deep Dive #5 · 2026-03-28
Source: Vitalik Buterin vs Beff Jezos/Guillaume Verdon debate · KB #11

One of the most technically fascinating threads in the debate is Vitalik's description of devices that use FHE — fully homomorphic encryption — to collect environmental data while preserving individual privacy. This technology is at the heart of d/acc's practical vision, and it's worth understanding why it matters.

Normal encryption protects data at rest and in transit. But the moment you need to actually compute on that data — aggregate it, analyze it, run machine learning on it — you have to decrypt it. And once it's decrypted, whoever has access to the server can see everything. This is why "we take your privacy seriously" is meaningless: if a company processes your data on their servers, they can see it.

Fully homomorphic encryption solves this by allowing computations directly on encrypted data. You encrypt your input, send it to a server, the server performs calculations on the ciphertext, and returns an encrypted result that — when you decrypt it — is exactly what you'd get if the computation had been done on the plaintext. The server never sees your data. Ever.

The concept was proposed by Rivest, Adleman, and Dertouzos in 1978, but the first working scheme wasn't constructed until Craig Gentry's breakthrough in 2009. Early FHE was absurdly slow — millions of times slower than plaintext computation. But the field has progressed dramatically. Modern schemes (like TFHE, BGV, CKKS) are approaching practical speeds for specific applications, and hardware acceleration is making it faster still.

Vitalik's air quality monitors are a perfect use case. You want city-wide air quality data (to detect pollution, pandemics, allergens). But you don't want anyone to know the exact air composition in your specific apartment — that reveals information about your cooking, smoking, health, and lifestyle. With FHE, each monitor encrypts its readings locally. A central server aggregates the encrypted data using homomorphic operations. The result — a city-wide air quality map — is collectively decrypted, but no individual monitor's data is ever exposed.

This is the d/acc vision made concrete: you get the benefits of mass data collection (safer cities, better public health) without the surveillance dystopia. It's defense-dominant technology — it makes it possible to have the sensors without the panopticon.

The limitations are real: FHE is still computationally expensive, the programming model is complex, and not all computations are easily expressed homomorphically. But the trajectory is clear. Combined with differential privacy (adding mathematical noise to prevent individual identification) and secure multi-party computation (splitting secrets across multiple parties), FHE is part of a cryptographic toolkit that could make Vitalik's vision of "sensors everywhere, surveillance nowhere" technically achievable within a decade.

For the crypto world, FHE is especially exciting because it enables private smart contracts — DeFi transactions where the amounts, participants, and logic are all encrypted but still verifiable. It's the missing piece between blockchain transparency and real-world privacy requirements.
